title: macOS persistence uses LOLBin-style command
why_it_matters: Potential persistence mechanism or suspicious autostart configuration invoking commonly abused interpreters or network tools.
false_positive_notes: Validate against approved management agents, software update tools, and enterprise login-item frameworks.
recommended_next_steps: Review the embedded evidence, inspect the referenced plist or autostart artifact, correlate with process and network evidence, and preserve the evidence bundle before remediation.

title: macOS persistence references temporary path
why_it_matters: Persistence that launches from temporary directories is suspicious because those locations are commonly used for staging malware.
false_positive_notes: Some installers and developer tools may temporarily reference these paths; validate persistence state and file provenance.
recommended_next_steps: Review the autostart item, inspect target file ownership/signing/quarantine attributes, and correlate with recent download or execution logs.

title: SSH authorized key material present
why_it_matters: SSH authorized keys can represent legitimate administration or attacker persistence.
false_positive_notes: Validate keys against approved admin key inventory and configuration management records.
recommended_next_steps: Review key comments, file ownership, mtime, provenance output, and authentication logs before removing keys.

title: Established network connection observed
why_it_matters: Established network sockets may indicate command-and-control, lateral movement, data transfer, or expected business traffic.
false_positive_notes: Many established connections are normal; validate process owner, destination, port, reputation, and timing.
recommended_next_steps: Correlate the process, destination, DNS, proxy, and firewall evidence before containment decisions.

title: Deleted-but-running file detected
why_it_matters: A deleted-but-running file can indicate evasive malware or a replaced executable still resident in memory.
false_positive_notes: Some package updates and temporary files can appear deleted while still open; validate process path, parent, and command line.
recommended_next_steps: Preserve process details, memory-relevant evidence, open file handles, and executable metadata before killing the process.

title: UID 0 account observed
why_it_matters: UID 0 accounts have root-equivalent privileges and may represent unauthorized privilege persistence.
false_positive_notes: The root account itself is expected; validate any additional UID 0 users or unusual shell/home combinations.
recommended_next_steps: Compare against a known-good account baseline and review account creation/modification logs.

title: Non-root UID 0 anomaly detected
why_it_matters: A non-root UID 0 user is a high-risk indicator of privilege persistence or account tampering.
false_positive_notes: Rare legacy appliances may use unusual UID 0 records; validate against asset-specific baselines.
recommended_next_steps: Preserve passwd/shadow/group evidence, correlate with recent account changes, and treat as high-priority containment input.

title: Linux LD preload persistence or rootkit signal
why_it_matters: LD preload abuse can intercept process execution and is common in userland rootkits.
false_positive_notes: Validate approved profiling, debugging, or EDR components before remediation.
recommended_next_steps: Preserve /etc/ld.so.preload, referenced libraries, hashes, process evidence, and timeline context before changing files.

title: Writable or suspicious systemd service path
why_it_matters: Writable service paths can let attackers replace or modify code executed by systemd.
false_positive_notes: Some custom services may be writable by design; validate ownership, permissions, and change history.
recommended_next_steps: Inspect unit files, drop-ins, ExecStart paths, ownership, package provenance, and recent modifications.

title: Unexpected Linux file capabilities
why_it_matters: Unexpected Linux capabilities can grant privilege-like behavior without setuid bits.
false_positive_notes: Some legitimate binaries use capabilities; compare against package manager and known-good baselines.
recommended_next_steps: Review the file path, package ownership, hash, mtime, and whether the capability is expected for that binary.

title: Suspicious PAM configuration indicator
why_it_matters: PAM changes can enable credential theft, authentication bypass, or persistence.
false_positive_notes: Some enterprise authentication stacks use custom PAM modules; validate against approved auth configuration.
recommended_next_steps: Preserve PAM configs, package ownership, recent file changes, and authentication logs before editing.

title: Recent package manager activity
why_it_matters: Recent package installs, upgrades, or removals can explain new binaries, services, or attacker tooling.
false_positive_notes: Normal patching creates package activity; correlate with maintenance windows and change tickets.
recommended_next_steps: Review package names, repositories, command lines, timing, and related service/process changes.

title: Container runtime artifact observed
why_it_matters: Container runtimes can host attacker tooling, persistence, or cryptomining workloads outside normal process review.
false_positive_notes: Containers may be expected on servers; validate images, mounts, privileges, networks, and owners.
recommended_next_steps: Review running containers, image provenance, privileged flags, mounts, restart policies, and recent image pulls.

title: Unsigned macOS code in suspicious context
pattern: not signed|code object is not signed|TeamIdentifier=not set
why_it_matters: Unsigned code in persistence or process context can indicate untrusted tooling or malware.
false_positive_notes: Internal scripts and developer tools may be unsigned; validate path, owner, quarantine, and business purpose.
recommended_next_steps: Inspect hashes, quarantine attributes, notarization/signing status, parent process, and persistence references.

title: macOS Gatekeeper/XProtect/MRT signal
why_it_matters: macOS security framework events can indicate blocked, scanned, or suspicious software execution.
false_positive_notes: Some events are routine scanning or benign blocks; validate event message, process, file path, and user action.
recommended_next_steps: Correlate with quarantine files, downloads, browser history, process evidence, and persistence artifacts.

title: macOS login item observed
why_it_matters: Login items can provide user-level persistence and may hide unwanted software.
false_positive_notes: Many enterprise and productivity apps use login items; validate developer identity and user approval.
recommended_next_steps: Review item path, hidden status, code signing, quarantine, ownership, and related background task management data.
